project: error types & quitting

data preparation & descriptives

  • data preparation
    • data cleaning
    • response time variables
      • totals
      • resp
  • most common errors
    • top_errors
    • entropy of error distributions
      • normalization
      • dominance
      • note:
      • plot individual error distributions
      • entropy and item rating
      • entropy and response time

data preparation

The current data ranges from 1st Sept 2021 to 31st May 2024.

I join the log data with item information from the dataset items. This dataset also comes with a table called duplicate_json. This resulted from some data checks in the item data, where some items are duplicated, but treated with different ids. This check was done in the scripts check_items.R and fix_json.R.

Then I do some initial data cleaning:

  • select only training set (training ids in folder rdata > ext).
  • create variables first, second, question_clean, response (factor), comma, answer_id, single_digit.
  • substitute the comma for a decimal in answer variable, so that it can be handled numerically.

I also make the table id_strings, which gives, per question_clean, the unique ids connected to it (since some have more than 1).

data cleaning

I remove:

  • items with less than 200 observations
  • learners with less than 100 observations
  • learners in grades 1 and 2

response time variables

I create the variables:

  • rt_fastslow in the log data. On a log level, was the response slower or faster than the median response time?
  • On an item level (grouped by question_clean):
  • rt_totalmean: average response time per item.
  • rt_totalsd: standard deviation of response times per item.
  • rt_mean: average response time, grouped by item and response type (correct, incorrect, skip).
  • rt_sd: standard deviation of response times, grouped by item and response type (correct, incorrect, skip).

totals

  • grouped by item
  • n of obs per response type
  • error rate = n_error / (n_cor + n_error)

resp

  • all responses, grouped by answer
resp %>% 
  filter(question_clean == "3 x 3") %>% 
  arrange(-n)

most common errors

top_errors

  • include only incorrect responses
  • group by answer
  • aggregate n per unique answer for top 10 rows per item

For example, for the item 9 x 9:

The top 10 most common errors among single-digit multiplication are:

4 x 0 = 4 (n = 754)
0 x 6 = 6 (n = 670)
3 x 3 = 6 (n = 661)
0 x 1 = 1 (n = 644)
4 x 4 = 12 (n = 323)
5 x 5 = 10 (n = 270)
8 x 5 = 45 (n = 262)
2 x 2 = 2 (n = 243)
8 x 4 = 24 (n = 235)
5 x 8 = 45 (n = 232)

entropy of error distributions

To quantify how concentrated or diffuse the error distributions are for each item, I compute the Shannon entropy of the item-specific error distribution.

For each item, the empirical distribution of incorrect answers is considered (plotted above).

The entropy of an item is defined as

\(H = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} p_k \log p_k\)

  • \(p_k\) denotes the proportion of errors resulting in answer \(k\).

  • \(K\) denotes the number of distinct incorrect answers.

normalization

Because the maximum possible entropy depends on \(K\), raw entropy values are not directly comparable across items. The maximum entropy for an item with \(K\) distinct errors is \(\log(K)\), which occurs when all incorrect answers are equally likely. I therefore normalize entropy by this maximum:

\(H_{norm} = \frac{H}{\log(K)}\)

This normalized entropy takes on values between 0 and 1, where 0 indicates that all errors fall on a single answer and 1 indicates a uniform distribution over the observed error answers.

dominance

For interpretation, I compute the inverse entropy measure, referred to as dominance:

$ = 1 - H_{norm} $

High dominance values correspond to sharply peaked error distributions, where one incorrect error dominates. Low dominance values correspond to flatter distributions with many competing error answers.

note:

  • Only incorrect answers are included in the entropy calculation. Skipped and late responses are not included.

  • Proportions (prop_error = n / n_error) are derived from the observed error counts for each item. Thus, probabilities used in the entropy calculation depend on relative frequencies. Indirectly, this implies that, for items with a higher total play count, these proportions are more stable, compared to items with a relatively smaller play count. Items with n < 200 are excluded from analysis.

errors <- resp %>% 
  filter(filter_error) %>% 
  arrange(first, second, desc(n)) %>% 
  mutate(prop_error = ifelse(response != "error", NA, n / n_error)) 

entropy <- errors %>%
  filter(filter_error) %>%
  group_by(question_clean) %>%
  summarise(
    single_digit = unique(single_digit),
    first = unique(first), 
    second = unique(second), 
    H = -sum(prop_error * log(prop_error), na.rm = TRUE),
    K = n(),
    H_norm = H / log(K),
    dominance = 1 - H_norm,
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# highest dominance items 
entropy %>% 
  filter(single_digit) %>% 
  arrange(-dominance) %>% 
  slice_head(n = 12) %>% 
  dplyr::select(question_clean, H, K, H_norm, dominance)
entropy <- entropy %>% 
  left_join(items %>% dplyr::select(question_clean, rating), by = "question_clean") %>%
  left_join(totals %>% dplyr::select(question_clean, n_qm, n_late, n_error), by = "question_clean") %>% 
  # remove duplicates
  distinct(question_clean, .keep_all = TRUE)

Looking at response distributions for high- and low-entropy items:

plot individual error distributions

entropy and item rating

Here I am looking at the relationship between entropy and item rating.

Some observations:

  • There is a clear positive association between rating and entropy: items with more dominance (more peaked distributions) tend to have lower rating estimates. In short, more difficult items elicit more structured error distrubtions.
    • interpretation: errors on easier items are often driven by a small number of salient, rule-based misconceptions which leads to strongly concentrated error distributions. because these items are otherwise easy, errors are rare but when they do occur, they tend to collapse onto a single dominant incorrect response. in contrast, more difficult items admit a larger space of plausible but incorrect strategies. this does not imply that difficult items are less informative, rather that no single erroneous procedure dominates across learners.
  • There a positive association between the absolute number of incorrect responses and dominance: items with higher dominance tend to accumulate a larger number of errors overall.
    • interpretation: this likely reflects the adaptivity of the system. easier items are played more, and these also show higher dominance.

entropy and response time

# create quantiles of dominance scores 
n_quant = 10
entropy <- entropy %>% 
  mutate(dom_quantile = factor(ntile(dominance, n_quant)))

groupsize <- entropy %>% 
  group_by(dom_quantile) %>% 
  count() %>% 
  ungroup() %>% 
  summarize(mean = mean(n)) %>% 
  pull(mean)

I separate dominance into quantiles, and then examine response time distributions of items across these quantiles. I also plot average and standard deviations of response times across items as a function of dominance.

n quantiles =  10 (average groupsize =  88.90909 )

ideas for entropy:

  • compute entropy separately by age group for the same item

    • possible relationships to look for: decreasing entropy with age (consistent with consolidation of error procedures) ; persistence of high dominance across ages (robust misconception?) ; disappearance of dominance with age (developmental phase error?)

    • interactions with rt?

  • compute person-specific (or day-specific) entropy across items?
